event_blocks.summary: A man in a dark suit and red tie, identified as James, initially sits at a warmly lit dressing room vanity surrounded by champagne glasses and a floral arrangement, but his pre-show atmosphere quickly shifts as backstage crew members cheer him on in a purple-lit hallway featuring framed photos and a neon 'The Late Late Show' sign. He ascends a short, industrial-style staircase towards a locked stage door labeled 'ST-2,' adorned with 'DO NOT ENTER' and 'RECORDING IN PROGRESS' signs, where he repeatedly attempts to open the handle and keypad, his frustration mounting with each failed attempt as a calm, synthesized voice provides incorrect passcodes. Exclaiming his disbelief and agitation, James protests, "I'm starting my final show in a... in a stairwell," but the voice insists, "The network insists. Show has to start." Despite his protests and attempts to welcome the audience and introduce guest Will Ferrell, loud thudding sounds indicate his continued struggle with the door, and he expresses his inability to see Ferrell due to the lack of a screen. After being informed Will is at the window, James hears Ferrell calling his name from behind the door, shouting "Up here!" James recognizes Ferrell and shouts back, "Hey!" and "I can't hear you!" through the door, his annoyance evident as he gestures, shakes his head, and leans against the unyielding barrier before descending the stairs and walking through the studio corridors, underscoring his urgent, yet thwarted, need to enter the restricted area.

His body language shifts from determined to exasperated, with hand gestures and hip placements indicating growing annoyance. He welcomes the audience, thanks his family, and introduces guest Will Ferrell, but expresses frustration at not being able to see him due to a lack of a screen for 'dressing room check-in.' He shouts through the door, trying to communicate with Will, and is heard making loud thudding sounds as he struggles with the door.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an upbeat, driving instrumental track as a man in a dark suit and red tie sits at a warmly lit vanity, surrounded by champagne glasses and a floral arrangement, suggesting a celebratory pre-show atmosphere. He then stands, prompting an immediate eruption of applause and enthusiastic cheers from the backstage crew members lining a narrow, purple-lit hallway. As he moves energetically, high-fiving and shaking hands, voices shout encouraging words like "Alright, hey!", "Come on!", "Yeah!", and "Woo!", all set against the continuing upbeat music. He ascends a short, industrial-style staircase towards a black door labeled 'ST-2', marked with stark warnings about cell phones and 'RECORDING IN PROGRESS'. The upbeat music continues as he attempts to open the door, a metallic clunk audible as he tries the locked handle multiple times. His initial excitement quickly turns to visible frustration as he exclaims, "What's going on? I can't get in. What's...?" An off-screen voice calmly states, "Problem with the door," to which the agitated man sharply retorts, "No, I can see that!" The Man in Suit, identified as James, stands before a black, industrial-style stage door labeled 'ST-2,' secured with a keypad lock. The door is adorned with multiple signs: a red-bordered notice about turning off cell phones, a 'DO NOT ENTER' sign, and a 'RECORDING IN PROGRESS' sign indicating 4:30-7:00 PM daily. James repeatedly attempts to open the door, first by trying the handle, then by pressing the keypad, his expressions shifting from focused determination to growing frustration. He asks, "What's the problem?" and a calm, synthesized voice responds, "Door's locked." James, exasperated, questions, "No, but why? Why is the door locked?" The voice explains, "Issue with the passcode." James insists he can do it, asking for the passcode. The voice provides "4 5 6 5," which James tries on the keypad, but the door remains locked, prompting him to declare, "No, it's not." The voice then suggests "4 5 6 6," which James also tries, again failing and stating, "No, it's not that, that's not it." As the voice offers "4 5 6 7," James, gesturing with his hands and placing them on his hips, asks incredulously, "Are you just making up numbers?" to which the voice calmly replies, "Yes." His frustration peaks as he exclaims, "Oh my god," and the voice, addressing him as "James," insists, "we have to start the show." James, looking back as if calling for help or expressing disbelief, protests, "No, I'm not... I'm starting my final show in a... in a stairwell." Despite his protests and declaration of "I don't care," the voice firmly reiterates, "The network insists. Show has to start." James, unable to gain entry, expresses his disbelief, "Oh my god, I can't believe this," as the door remains firmly locked throughout, underscoring the barrier in the stark, white-walled corridor.

The video opens with James, the host of "The Late Late Show," in a dark suit and red tie, visibly frustrated as he repeatedly attempts to open a locked stage door labeled 'ST-2'. A calm, off-screen voice states "Standby," followed by another thud, then informs James, "Will is at the window." James responds with a confused "What?" as the voice repeats the information. James recognizes "Will!" and shouts "Hey!" and "I can't hear you!" through the door, muttering in confusion as another thud sounds. Displaying his annoyance, he places his hands on his hips, shakes his head, points at the unyielding barrier, leans against the door, and peers through its small window before turning away in frustration. He then walks down the short industrial-style staircase, glancing back at the door, and continues through a narrow corridor with purple lighting and framed photos, eventually reaching a hallway with a neon sign reading 'The Late Late Show,' underscoring the television studio setting and his urgent, yet thwarted, need to enter the restricted area.

event_blocks.summary: A Man in a dark suit, later identified as James Corden, initially struggles with a locked stage door labeled 'ST-2', repeatedly trying the keypad and peering through the window, expressing frustration and exclaiming, "Absolute disaster." A Man in a Yellow Shirt inside waves, adding to his exasperation. After a voice suggests using a fire axe, a calm voice announces the "Door issue has been resolved" and provides the passcode "1111," which James finds unbelievable given his eight-year tenure. He then rushes through the door into a dimly lit backstage area where Backstage Crew Members cheer, and proceeds onto a stage where a Live Band performs an energetic rock song under warm lighting, with the Audience clapping and cheering. The segment transitions to James Corden confidently emerging from a curtain to a roaring crowd, thanking them and asking them to be seated. He then announces, "I'm James Corden for the last time. This, this is The Late Late Show! Roll the titles!" The Live Band plays an upbeat theme song accompanying a montage of James Corden riding a motorcycle with glowing wheels, visiting a ZOLTAR Fortune-Telling Booth, and driving a red convertible through a city at night, culminating in an aerial shot of a building with 'THE LATE LATE SHOW WITH JAMES CORDEN' signage. The show concludes with James Corden seated at his Desk with Mug against a Cityscape Background, humorously stating, "we are determined to get it right this time," and introducing his family, including his mother, who engages in playful banter about botox and collagen. He then shifts to a serious tone, declaring their "sacred duty to give you, the American people, the biggest, most important news stories."

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Suit, James Corden, began with a monologue to the audience, which erupted in laughter, before the scene shifted to the Man in Brown Jacket at a desk with a mug reading 'DON'T TALK TO ME UNTIL I'M READY.' Dramatic orchestral music introduced President Joe Biden, who formally congratulated "James" on "1200 shows in eight years," prompting exaggerated surprise from the Man in Suit in an inset. Biden thanked "Reggie and the entire team" for "all the joy you've brought to homes across America," causing the Man in Suit to slump over his desk as the music swelled. The scene transitioned to a staged Oval Office where the Man in Suit presented flowers to President Biden, who gave a thumbs-up. This was followed by a live stage performance with the Man in Suit and a musician, and a wide shot of 'The Late Late Show' cast and crew posing on stage amidst audience laughter and applause. A montage of surreal clips featured the Man in Suit in a superhero costume with Tom Cruise, dancing sailors in New York City, and a golden bust in a car. The video returned to President Biden speaking formally, with the Man in Suit in disbelief, as Biden delivered a final joke about not being asked to sing in a car, concluding with a quick transition sound. In a subsequent surreal Oval Office scene, President Biden and the Man in Suit, both wearing sunglasses and holding cups, engaged in mock-serious conversation, with Biden stating, "We can't say goodbye today," and "We'll miss you, pal. I can't wait to see what you do next." A 'whoosh' sound marked the transition back to 'The Late Late Show' set, where the Man in Suit, initially with his head down, broke into an animated smile, exclaiming, "Um, that is amazing," and humorously questioning if he had died while watching photos. He then requested a clip from Trump, eliciting more laughter and cheering. The Live Band, including a keyboardist in a colorful shirt and sunglasses and a female musician, was shown clapping and smiling as Corden announced a surprise after the break, calling it the "last time we'll ever do this." An energetic band music segment followed, with Corden hyping the crowd. The Man in Suit continued his monologue with expressive gestures, entertaining the audience, including a woman in a leopard-print scarf and a bald man in glasses, under dynamic stage lighting with a Cityscape Background. The segment ended with the Man in Suit laughing heartily, hands raised in celebration, as the audience erupted in applause and continued energetic band music.

Dramatic, fanfare-like orchestral music begins as the narrative shifts to President Joe Biden speaking formally before American flags. Biden's voice congratulates "James" on an "incredible run" of "1200 shows in eight years." The Man in Suit, in a small inset, reacts with exaggerated surprise, comically interjecting, "This is both a snack and a gift," and later asking, "Who is that guy?" after Biden expresses surprise at his longevity. Biden then thanks "Reggie and the entire team" for "all the joy you've brought to homes across America," as the Man in Suit slumps over his desk. The orchestral music swells. The scene transitions to a staged Oval Office where the Man in Suit, in casual attire, presents flowers to President Biden, who gives a thumbs-up. The video then cuts to a live stage performance with the Man in Suit and a musician with an afro and sunglasses smiling, followed by a wide shot of 'The Late Late Show' cast and crew posing on stage, all accompanied by audience laughter and applause. A montage of comedic and surreal clips follows: the Man in Suit in a blue superhero costume with Tom Cruise, dancing sailors in New York City, and a golden bust in a car. The final frames show the Man in Suit and a woman in a car reacting with shock and excitement, before cutting back to President Biden speaking formally, with the Man in Suit again in an inset, hands raised in disbelief, as Biden delivers a final joke: "And a special thanks for never asking me to sing in a car." A quick transition sound concludes the segment.

The video opens with a surreal and comedic scene in the Oval Office, where President Joe Biden and the Man in Suit (James Corden) are seated in armchairs, both wearing sunglasses and holding steaming cups, engaging in a mock-serious conversation. A solemn instrumental piece plays as President Joe Biden's voice is heard saying, "We can't say goodbye today," followed by, "We'll miss you, pal. I can't wait to see what you do next." The scene quickly cuts to President Joe Biden in a formal address, standing in front of American flags, speaking with expressive hand gestures. A distinct 'whoosh' sound marks the transition to the set of 'The Late Late Show with James Corden,' where the Man in Suit is initially seen with his head down on his desk, appearing exhausted or defeated, before lifting his head and breaking into a wide, animated smile. He gestures energetically, speaking to the audience with enthusiasm, his red tie and dark suit clearly visible, exclaiming, "Um, that is amazing." He humorously recounts, "That was amazing, although there was a minute in the middle when I was watching that where I, when those photos were going, I was like, 'Wait, have I died?'" eliciting audience laughter. He continues, "Wait, we also have one... Oh, no, don't do this. Oh, no, I can't. What? We also have one from Trump. Go ahead and roll it," prompting more laughter and some cheering. The camera pans to the Live Band, showing a keyboardist in a colorful patterned shirt and sunglasses and a female musician clapping, both smiling and engaged, as Corden announces, "Listen! After the break, we got a big surprise for you. Don't go anywhere. This is the last time we'll ever do this. Come on back, everybody!" This leads into an energetic, upbeat band music segment, with Corden's voice heard over the music, hyping up the crowd. The Man in Suit continues his monologue, using expressive hand movements and facial expressions, clearly entertaining the audience, who are shown clapping and laughing, with a woman in a leopard-print scarf and a bald man in glasses visibly enjoying the performance. The stage is lit with warm, dynamic lighting, and the Cityscape Background is visible behind the desk. The segment ends with the Man in Suit laughing heartily, his hands raised in celebration, as the audience erupts in applause and continued energetic band music, capturing the lively and joyful atmosphere of the show.

event_blocks.summary: A live band energetically performs before the Man in Suit, James, addresses the audience from his desk, reflecting on the show's end and desiring sleep, met with laughter. He then appears in his bedroom in polka-dot pajamas, snoring, and is awakened by a New Man in Blue Shirt holding a yellow flower, who reveals they need to discuss James leaving the talk show. A New Man in Black Suit joins, explaining the exclusivity of hosting a late-night show and the need to keep its secrets. In the bedroom, James discusses secrets and borrowing a mouthguard, prompting confusion from the New Man in Blue Shirt. The New Man in Black Suit, now also in pajamas, expresses frustration about watching "Mozart in the Jungle" unnecessarily, a sentiment echoed by the New Man in Gray Suit who enters and states Mozart was never in the jungle, leading to Steven lamenting missing his sister's wedding. James then reveals the "fake laugh" secret, causing shared laughter. The New Man in Gray Suit confirms Steven is leaving and James lives there, introducing "James Corden, everybody!" James Corden pleads for them to leave, promising secrecy despite their home invasion. The New Man in Blue Shirt questions if James has considered the implications of leaving a job that allows nightly song and dance, which James concedes has been "a lot." The scene shifts to a tense bedroom conversation where the New Man in Black Suit, agitated, declares he is leaving after being told he performed a musical number about Covid being over, despite a brief cutaway showing a celebration and "no Lockdowns anymore." The New Man in Gray Suit dictates terms for his exit, including not appearing to enjoy retirement and growing a specific beard, while an Older Man with a White Beard shares a brief reflection on divine communication. Back in the bedroom, the New Man in Black Suit, in pajamas, argues they are a family spreading joy, but the New Man in Gray Suit sternly instructs him to stick to corporate gigs and podcasts, and later tells him he's embarrassing his country. As James apologizes, the New Man in Gray Suit proposes discussing the division of ideas and assets from his departing show. All men receive white envelopes, examining them as a chorus declares "Carpool Karaoke," to which the pajama-clad man expresses surprise at the lack of other "bits," met with skepticism.

Steven, now standing and gesturing defensively, laments missing his "sister's wedding for that!" The New Man in Gray Suit addresses Steven by name, asking if he's leaving, to which James replies he lives there. The New Man in Gray Suit then introduces "James Corden, everybody!" event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit, modern bedroom, the New Man in Black Suit, wearing polka-dot pajamas and holding a Met Gala invitation, engaged in a heated discussion with the New Man in Gray Suit, proposing "emoji news" before defensively asserting ownership of his ideas and refusing to grow a beard or adopt certain styles. event_blocks.summary: James Corden, dressed in a dark suit, light blue shirt, and red tie, opens 'The Late Late Show' by enthusiastically telling the audience they will be shocked by how the show ends, teasing a musical finale. Following a drum roll, he announces a song, to cheers. The scene dramatically shifts to Corden at a grand piano center stage, bathed in warm spotlights and smoke effects, singing an emotional tenor accompanied by piano and strings. He sings about the show's end, mentioning the last guest, conversations, and rehearsals, and humorously states, "No more late late Tinder for swiping," which cuts to a man in a pink vest reacting comically before a giant 'late late live tinder' screen. The song continues as a piñata is smashed with a bat, releasing confetti, followed by a surreal shot of a bird flying through an overexposed sky. The song then features Corden singing about squeezing into spanks, intercut with a split-screen of a man in a 'The Late Late Show' t-shirt smiling at a monitor and a shirtless man on a boat. Back on stage, Corden sings about the last Carpool Karaoke, humorously intercut with him and a woman screaming in a car interior, and the last 'spill your guts' segment, intercut with a woman eating from a bizarre food tray. He expresses his impending return to London, lamenting leaving LA, its weather, friends, and microgreen salads. The performance returns to the stage with emotional intensity, close-ups, and sweeping lights, before concluding with a wide shot of the piano and choir in a hazy, golden glow as he sings, "And Reggie will..." The narrative then shifts to Corden at the grand piano, singing "no longer ask people questions that aren't the least bit apropos," with a live band providing a melancholic backdrop. He transitions to a comedic spoken interlude, asking "What is the nature of time?" and eliciting laughter. Returning to song, he sings a reflective closing, "So goodnight. Thanks for watching. That's our show," and pays tribute to the crew with a montage of behind-the-scenes moments. Corden concludes his heartfelt message, "I'm grateful for the eight years I spent with them. And the eight years I spent here with you," in the intimate yet grand performance setting, with warm, dramatic lighting enhancing the mood, concluding with a final wide shot of the full ensemble and audience in the atmospheric glow, accompanied by the band's sustained, reflective music.

event_blocks.summary: On a dimly lit, hazy stage, James Corden, clad in a dark blue suit, white shirt, and red tie, sat at a glossy black grand piano, singing passionately into a microphone under dramatic, warm spotlights. Behind him, a choir of performers stood in silhouette, their harmonies blending with Corden's melancholic singing and the piano's melody, while stage lighting fixtures cast golden beams. Corden continued, "Yet I know as I look in the camera for the very last time as your host, that each of those thousands of moments of joy is the one I'll remember the most." He reflected, "And part of me thinks why not stay here forever, but deep in my heart I just know... No more shows to be showing. It's time I was going. It's time. Thanks for watching. That's our show." As he finished, the audience, visible as attentive silhouettes, erupted in applause. Corden then addressed the clapping and cheering audience, "everybody, I will miss you. Goodnight," before standing and walking down the center aisle, waving to the standing crowd as an orchestral instrumental piece played. Reaching the stage edge, he offered a final wave before exiting through a curtain beneath 'THE LATE LATE SHOW' marquee, with the audience's applause and cheers persisting. The scene transitioned to end credits, including 'EXECUTIVE PRODUCER BRIAN TILLY' and 'EXECUTIVE PRODUCER JAMES CORDEN,' rolling over the audience as the music faded, concluding with the 'fulwell73 PRODUCTIONS' and 'CBS BROADCASTING INC.' logos against a dark, blurred background with lens flares.
